pair.title: Alberta startup sells no-tech tractors for half price

story.title: This Alberta Startup Sells No-Tech Tractors for Half Price

comments.text: This is going to reshape small-scale farming way more than people realize. I spent five years working on a dairy operation in rural Ontario, and the repair costs on modern equipment are absolutely brutal. A single software glitch on a 2019 John Deere costs $3,500 for a dealer diagnostic, then another $8,000-$12,000 to replace whatever module failed. Meanwhile, a farmer can pull a Cummins injector pump off a '95 pickup at a salvage yard for $300

kids.text:
This is exactly the gap the market needed. I've been following the right-to-repair movement for a couple years now, and what strikes me is how many farmers are stuck: modern equipment is genuinely better engineered, but the TCO becomes insane once you factor in dealer lock-in and software failures.

The Cummins P-pump approach is brilliant because it's not nostalgic or anti-technology, it's just refusing to pay for unnecessary complexity. A 12-valve mechanical injection system has been battle-tested for 30+ years. You can diagnose it with a multimeter. Parts are commodity items, not proprietary modules with firmware handshakes.

The 400 inquiries from the US after one interview tells you something real is happening. I

comments.text:
There's a similar project called Open Source Ecology that's been pushing this exact angle for over a decade. They've built documentation for completely serviceable farm equipment, focusing on modularity and zero vendor lock-in. The tractor designs are all open source on GitHub.

Ursa Ag is smart to go the remanufactured-engine route though, since actually *producing* diesel engines at scale is a capital nightmare. OSE ran into that wall hard. The real win here is that farmers are finally getting a credible alternative when John Deere's business model is basically "you rent the tractor, we own the software."

https://opensourceecology.org

kids.text: OSE's documentation push was genuinely valuable, but they never solved the unit economics problem. You need volume to amortize tooling costs, and open-source farm equipment attracts builders, not buyers at scale. Ursa's doing something harder: they're actually selling them. 400 inquiries from a single interview suggests the market was waiting

The fact that this is getting 400 inquiries and still feels like news in 2024 is the real story. We shouldn't need a scrappy Alberta startup to remind us that farmers want to own and fix their own equipment. The fact that it's novel enough to be noteworthy says everything about

OSE's modular designs never hit market scale partly because the engineering was over-specified. Ursa's smarter constraint: don't reinvent the engine, just strip the electronics. That 5.9L Cummins is bulletproof because it's been in the field for 30

The right-to-repair angle is valid, but I'm noticing HN comments always frame this as David vs. Goliath when the actual story is simpler: farmers want cheaper tractors, and Ursa found a business model that works. That's not a moral victory against Big Ag, that's just market segmentation.

Open Source Ecology's been around since 2003 and has exactly zero production tractors in the field. Meanwhile Ursa shipped 400 inquiries from a single interview. The comparison does OSE a disservice because they're solving a completely different problem (educational modularity) than Ursa (sell a working tractor for half price). One is aspirational, the other is commercial. Both are fine, but conflating them muddles what's actually happening.

The real constraint Ursa is hitting isn't philosophically interesting: you can't buy a new 12-valve Cummins anymore because emissions. So they

comments.text: The right-to-repair angle is real, but the actual innovation here is simpler: they're betting farmers will trade modern diagnostics for mechanical simplicity and price. A 12-valve Cummins with a Bosch P-pump is bulletproof because there's almost nothing to break.

kids.text: Exactly right. The Cummins 12-valve is nearly indestructible precisely because it's old enough to predate the reliability-killing complexity creep. No turbo boost controller, no EGR cooler failures, no DPF regeneration cycles. A farmer can diagnose a

The 12-valve Cummins is bulletproof, but let's be precise about why. It's not just old age-it's the architecture. A mechanical P-pump doesn't need calibration or software updates. The injection timing is fixed by cam geometry. Compare that to a modern common-rail system where the ECU adjusts timing thousands of times per second based on sensor inputs. More control, yes, but also more failure modes.

That said, the real win for Ursa isn't nostalgia. It's that farmers can actually own their repair ecosystem. A P-pump failure? There are thousands of rebuilders. A failed modern injector? You're calling the dealer and waiting three weeks. The economics flip hard once you factor in downtime during harvest.

I'd be

Cory Doctorow has been hammering this exact nail for five years now, and it's wild that it takes a Canadian startup undercutting Deere by 50% to make the tech press actually listen. The real story isn't the tractor, it's that we've accepted "

comments.text: The Bosch P-pump detail is doing a lot of work here, but there's a gotcha buried in the spec: mechanical injection systems still need injectors serviced regularly, and Cummins 5.9L injector cups are prone to cavitation erosion. You're swapping one proprietary headache (John Deere's firmware lockdown) for another (injector replacement intervals of 8,000-12

kids.text: I've been running Deutz air-cooled diesels on our family farm for 15 years, and honestly the serviceability argument cuts both ways. Yes, you avoid the John Deere nightmare. But Cummins 5.9L injector work isn't trivial either-you

IIRC the 5.9L Cummins injector cups are actually pretty robust on the mechanical P-pump setup, the cavitation issue shows up more with the common-rail stuff. Bosch P-pumps run lower injection pressures (~17k psi vs 23k+), which

You're right that the mechanical P-pump setup handles lower pressures better, but I'd push back slightly on the robustness claim. I've seen plenty of 5.9L Cummins with cavitation pitting in the injector cups, even on the older mechanical systems. The difference is that on a P-pump truck, you're looking at maybe $400-600 in parts and a weekend with a socket set. On common-rail, yeah, you're into dealer territory and four figures fast.

The real advantage Ursa is selling here isn't that mechanical injection is bulletproof (it isn't), it's that when it fails, a farmer can actually fix it without calling Deere's software department. An 8,000-hour injector cup replacement on a mechanical system is annoying but knowable. You can budget for

comments.text: This reminds me of the broader shift happening in agricultural equipment toward simplicity. There's a whole parallel market emerging around pre-1990s combines and balers that farmers are actively seeking out and refurbishing, partly because the mechanical systems are genuinely more repairable but also because parts availability for older iron

I'm skeptical this solves the real problem. Sure, you get a cheaper tractor, but what happens when that remanufactured Cummins engine needs work in five years? You're betting everything on finding a diesel mechanic willing to work on a 1990s architecture, not a John Deere service network. The price advantage evaporates fast if you're paying $150/hr labor rates in rural Alberta for someone who has to figure out your machine from first principles. Has

kids.text: You're right that service becomes critical. We've actually been talking to mechanics across the prairies about this, turns out there's still decent appetite for 90s Cummins work, partly because fleet operators kept those engines in service longer than most realize. That said, we're not naive about it. We're building a parts compatibility guide and working with a handful of independent shops to document common maintenance intervals. It won't match Deere's dealer density, but it's more than zero. The real bet is that simplicity itself becomes the advantage, fewer things break, and when they do, it's closer to small-engine repair than proprietary diagnostics. We're also committing to keeping detailed schematics public. Not a complete solution to your point, but we

I had a 1995 Cummins in a Dodge pickup that ran for 340,000 miles with basic maintenance. Sold it to a farmer who needed it for his operation, and he still messages me about it ten years later. The thing about those engines is they're genuinely simple to keep alive if you're willing to do the work yourself or know someone who understands diesel fundamentals rather than computer diagnostics.

The real advantage here isn't just price, it's agency. My neighbor has a newer John Deere and he can't even change his own oil without the dealer's blessing (software-wise). With one of these Ursa tractors, you own the machine in a way that's becoming rare. A local mechanic told me
